I think @tobes may have a branch to deal with this stuff. What is more is that the formatters can do more than just hiding zeroes... IIRC it can do `\?if=max_length 1`, `\?if=min_length 1`, `\?not_zero`, `\?if=placeholder`, `{placeholder:d}` (decimal), `{placeholder:.0f}` (rounding a float), and can grow more later. Maybe we will get it documented for `3.8` or `3.9`, but will be easier to fix this module in the `format` now rather than to try and fix it it later / deprecate.

Two things I wonder about this:

1) Can we safely put this before `notify`? It'd be nice to put all of the object params before the `notify` param, since the latter isn't really related to business logic. The question is, how many people use `notify`, and how many of those use it as a positional argument? Even if we're worried about that, we can still make it as a breaking change, since we're in the transition to 2.0.0.

2) Rather than stacking a bunch of optional params at the end (it's only `can_view_path` for now, but who knows, in the future there could be others), I wonder if we should instead expose a generic parameter for extra body params. Something like `(additional | extra | optional)_attributes`. And then callers could do `additional_attributes={'can_view_path': True, 'foo': 'bar'}`

Regarding replacing multiplication (FMA) to incremental addition (+=), two points of my view:

1)  The incremental addition is more concise and logical rather than computing the index from start point each time by multiplication. But there're strong compute dependency and we **CANT** start the loop N+1 before the loop N. 
Thus, we have to change to multiple styles for the parallelization.

2) The efficiency of Multiplication (FMA) and addition (ADD) is same in the latest HW (Intel skylake)
Take SSE instruct as an example:
ADD, latency 4, CPI 0.5;
https://software.intel.com/sites/landingpage/IntrinsicsGuide/#text=add&techs=SSE&expand=127,3673,127
FMA (MUL+ADD), latency 4, CPI 0.5
https://software.intel.com/sites/landingpage/IntrinsicsGuide/#expand=127,3673,2395,3673,2395,2407&text=mul&techs=FMA,Other

Maybe caused by my ignorance, I see some details here that leave this open to confusion and errors:
- The query uses `FROM stdin` but the function name is `copyfrom_file_path`.
- The file is a .csv and then you also specify the `FORMAT`.
- The query is a `COPY`, and the function is, as well.

Can't Python code be less coupled to the query? Example:

```python
result = copy_client.copyfrom_file_path('copy_example', ['the_geom', 'name', 'age'])
```
By specifying the target table and the columns you can then compose the query. There could also be named parameters with good defaults. In my previous example, `FORMAT` is inferred from the extension and `HEADER` defaults to true, but it could become explicit if needed: 

```python
result = copy_client.copyfrom_file_path('copy_example', ['the_geom', 'name', 'age'], format='text', header=False)
```

I think using undocumented functionality is serious enough to warrant too many reminders rather than too little. People will likely skip the long comment and just stumble over the in-line 'undocumented' one, which will then prompt them to read the comment above. Alternatively, they'll read the long comment, misunderstand it, notice the undocumented in-line and re-read the long comment.

One small issue that no one has bothered to fix (and maybe that's OK) is that the anchor section (`#...`) comes last in a URL
http://www.example.com:80/path/to/myfile.html?key1=value1&key2=value2#SomewhereInTheDocument
so if the base URL has an anchor, we want to make sure to append the GET params to the left of `#`.

However, TensorBoard logic never includes an anchor within a base URL. It wouldn't work because of course we sync the hash value with the current dashboard. I guess this is mainly an FYI for now.
